items.html:
Human rights belong to everyone

Equality law is not the only protection the people you support rely on. The Human Rights Act 1998 gives everyone in the UK a set of basic rights simply because they are human — rights that cannot be earned, and cannot be taken away because of someone’s age, illness or where they live.

The Act brings key rights into UK law and requires public bodies, including many care providers, to respect them. The rights that matter most in care include the right to life, freedom from inhuman or degrading treatment, respect for private and family life, and freedom of thought, conscience and religion.

The FREDA principles

Human rights can feel abstract, so a simple framework called FREDA helps you put them into everyday practice. Each letter stands for a value that should run through the care you give:

  • Fairness: follow fair, open processes and treat people even-handedly.
  • Respect: value each person’s views, wishes and way of life.
  • Equality: make sure no one is disadvantaged because of who they are.
  • Dignity: never treat a person as an object or a task to be completed.
  • Autonomy: support people to make their own choices and stay in control.

items.question: You are helping Mr Osei, who lives with dementia, to wash and dress. A colleague suggests rushing him and choosing his clothes for him to save time. Which FREDA principles does this risk breaching?

answers.text: Dignity and autonomy, because it treats him as a task and removes his choices.
answers.feedback: Correct. Rushing him and deciding for him strips away his dignity and his right to stay in control of his own care.

answers.text: None, because saving time is always in the person’s best interests.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Saving time does not justify overriding someone’s dignity or removing their choices.

answers.text: Only fairness, because everyone is treated the same quick way.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Treating everyone the same rushed way is not fairness; it ignores dignity and autonomy.

answers.text: Human rights do not apply to people living with dementia.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Human rights belong to everyone, including people living with dementia.

items.html:
Communication that includes everyone

Inclusive communication means sharing information in ways each person can understand and respond to. If someone cannot follow what you say, or cannot read what you give them, they cannot make choices, raise concerns or take part in their own care — and that quickly becomes a form of exclusion.

People have very different needs. Someone may be Deaf and use British Sign Language, have a learning disability, be blind or partially sighted, or find spoken English difficult. Good practice is to find out how each person prefers to give and receive information, and then to provide it that way.

The Accessible Information Standard

Since 2016, all organisations that provide NHS or adult social care in England must follow the Accessible Information Standard. It sets out how you should meet the communication needs of people with a disability, impairment or sensory loss. There are five clear steps:

  • Ask: identify whether a person has any information or communication needs.
  • Record: note those needs clearly and consistently.
  • Flag: make the needs highly visible in the person’s file.
  • Share: pass the needs on to others involved, with the person’s consent.
  • Act: meet the needs, for example with large print, Easy Read or an interpreter.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these sentences about the Accessible Information Standard.
items.textField: The Accessible Information Standard applies to people with a disability, impairment or sensory *loss*. The first step is to *ask* whether a person has any communication needs. You then *record* those needs and *flag* them clearly in the file. Information can be made accessible using *Easy* Read, large print or an interpreter.

items.html:
People are more than one label

The Equality Act lists nine protected characteristics, but no one is defined by a single one. A person is never just their age, or just their religion — they hold several identities at the same time. Intersectionality is the idea that these identities overlap and shape a person’s experience together.

For example, a young disabled woman may face barriers that are different from those met by a disabled man, or by a woman who is not disabled. Her experience is not simply disability added to gender; the two combine in ways that are unique to her.

Why this matters in your care

If you think about only one part of who someone is, you can miss the barriers that really affect them. An older gay man may worry about being open with staff; a Black woman may have had past experiences that make her wary of services. Seeing the whole person helps you give care that truly fits.

  • See the whole person: notice how someone’s identities work together, not in isolation.
  • Avoid assumptions: two people who share one characteristic are not the same.
  • Listen first: let each person tell you what matters most to them.

items.question: Intersectionality means that a person’s overlapping identities — such as age, race, disability and gender — combine to shape their experience, so you should see the whole person rather than a single characteristic.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Identities overlap and interact, so recognising the whole person helps you spot the barriers that truly affect them.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Intersectionality is about identities overlapping and combining, so focusing on just one characteristic can hide the barriers a person really faces.

items.html:
Culture, religion and belief in daily life

Religion or belief is a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, and this includes having no religion. Culture, faith and belief shape many everyday things: what people eat, how they dress, when and how they pray or observe festivals, and their wishes around personal care, illness and dying.

Supporting these is not an optional extra. When you help someone follow what matters to them — a halal or kosher meal, a quiet space to pray, or modest dress during personal care — you protect their dignity and their rights.

Ask, do not assume

It is easy to guess someone’s needs from their name, background or appearance, but assumptions often go wrong. People of the same faith practise in very different ways, and some observe little or nothing at all. The only reliable guide is the person themselves.

  • Ask respectfully: find out what each person wants recorded in their care plan.
  • Avoid stereotypes: do not treat a whole community as if everyone is the same.
  • Support, do not judge: your own beliefs must not shape the care you give.
  • Plan ahead: know about festivals or fasting that may affect meals and routines.

items.question: A new person you support has an Arabic name. What is the best way to meet their religious and cultural needs?

answers.text: Ask the person what matters to them and record their wishes, without assuming anything from their name.
answers.feedback: Correct. The person themselves is the only reliable guide; asking and recording their wishes avoids harmful assumptions.

answers.text: Arrange halal meals and a prayer mat straight away, because that is what their name suggests.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Guessing from a name is a stereotype; people of the same background practise in very different ways.

answers.text: Wait for them to complain before making any changes.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Waiting for a complaint leaves the person’s needs unmet; you should ask from the start.

answers.text: Treat everyone exactly the same and offer no adjustments at all.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Treating everyone identically ignores individual needs and can breach their dignity and rights.

items.html:
You are not expected to know everything

Equality, diversity and inclusion can raise questions you cannot answer alone, and that is completely normal. Knowing where to turn is part of doing the job well, and help is closer than you might think — starting inside your own organisation.

  • Your line manager: usually the first person to ask about a concern or about someone’s needs.
  • Workplace policies: your equality, diversity and inclusion policy sets out what is expected of everyone.
  • Care plans and colleagues: a valuable source of what already works well for a person.

Independent and specialist support

Some questions need support from beyond your immediate team. Knowing these sources means no one has to struggle in silence:

  • Advocacy services: an independent advocate helps a person understand their rights and speak up for what they want.
  • Human resources or a trade union: can advise you if you experience or witness discrimination at work.
  • National organisations: the Equality and Human Rights Commission and ACAS offer clear guidance on rights and fair treatment.

items.question: A person you support, Priya, tells you she feels left out of group activities because of her religion, and she is unsure of her rights. What is the most helpful response?

answers.text: Listen, reassure her, tell your manager, and explain that an independent advocate can help her understand her rights and be heard.
answers.feedback: Correct. Listening, reporting to your manager and offering advocacy all help Priya be heard and understand her rights.

answers.text: Tell her not to worry and that she is probably imagining it.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Dismissing her concern ignores a possible case of discrimination and leaves her unsupported.

answers.text: Deal with it yourself and keep it away from your manager to avoid a fuss.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Concerns like this should be shared with your manager, not hidden away.

answers.text: Suggest she leaves the service if she is unhappy.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The answer is to address the exclusion and uphold her rights, not to push her out of the service.
